coredata.dc:title: An efficient removal of methylene blue dye by adsorption onto carbon dot @ zinc peroxide embedded poly vinyl alcohol (PVA/CZnO2) nano-composite: A novel Reusable adsorbent
coredata.dc:creator: El-Shamy, Ahmed Gamal
coredata.dc:description: Both novel nano-composites based on zinc peroxide (ZnO2) and carbon dots (CDots) decorated zinc peroxide (CZnO2) embedded in polyvinyl alcohol, (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2) films, have been successfully designed for pollutant dye removal. The obtained films are scrutinized by using XRD, BET, TEM and SEM microscope. SEM micrograph shows that both nano-composites have good dispersion of nano-particles and some pore structures. PVA/CZnO2 has a high surface area of (241.04 m2 g-1) higher than PVA/ZnO2 (167.17 m2 g-1), according to BET analysis, which gives it the opportunity to be a good catalyst. PVA/CZnO2 catalyst displays ultra-high adsorption capacity against methylene blue (MB). More excitingly, at the same concentration (4 wt%) of each filler, the PVA/CZnO2 catalyst shows the largest adsorption capacity (1972 ± 40 mg/g) where PVA/ZnO2 (1831 ± 20 mg/g) proves the important role of CDots on the adsorption process. More interestingly, 40 mg was the optimum dosage of (PVA/CZnO2) catalyst, which was able to remove MB dye with efficiency up to 98%. The supreme removal efficiency (>98%) of 2000 mg/L of MB dye has been quickly obtained (in 60 min) at the experimental conditions. Langmuir type I model is the adsorption mechanism suggesting MB dye mono-layer formation on the surface of catalyst. Chemisorption is the interaction type between the catalyst and molecules of dye, according to the kinetic model of pseudo-second-order. 75% of loaded-dye is desorbed from the catalyst by soaking it in a solution of pH = 1, according to desorption tests. The reused experiments confirm that the catalyst can be reused for at least 5 runs with minimal loss in its efficiency (~2%).

head.ce:title: An efficient removal of methylene blue dye by adsorption onto carbon dot @ zinc peroxide embedded poly vinyl alcohol (PVA/CZnO2) nano-composite: A novel Reusable adsorbent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Both novel nano-composites based on zinc peroxide (ZnO2) and carbon dots (CDots) decorated zinc peroxide (CZnO2) embedded in polyvinyl alcohol, (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2) films, have been successfully designed for pollutant dye removal. The obtained films are scrutinized by using XRD, BET, TEM and SEM microscope. SEM micrograph shows that both nano-composites have good dispersion of nano-particles and some pore structures. PVA/CZnO2 has a high surface area of (241.04 m2 g-1) higher than PVA/ZnO2 (167.17 m2 g-1), according to BET analysis, which gives it the opportunity to be a good catalyst. PVA/CZnO2 catalyst displays ultra-high adsorption capacity against methylene blue (MB). More excitingly, at the same concentration (4 wt%) of each filler, the PVA/CZnO2 catalyst shows the largest adsorption capacity (1972 ± 40 mg/g) where PVA/ZnO2 (1831 ± 20 mg/g) proves the important role of CDots on the adsorption process. More interestingly, 40 mg was the optimum dosage of (PVA/CZnO2) catalyst, which was able to remove MB dye with efficiency up to 98%. The supreme removal efficiency (>98%) of 2000 mg/L of MB dye has been quickly obtained (in 60 min) at the experimental conditions. Langmuir type I model is the adsorption mechanism suggesting MB dye mono-layer formation on the surface of catalyst. Chemisorption is the interaction type between the catalyst and molecules of dye, according to the kinetic model of pseudo-second-order. 75% of loaded-dye is desorbed from the catalyst by soaking it in a solution of pH = 1, according to desorption tests. The reused experiments confirm that the catalyst can be reused for at least 5 runs with minimal loss in its efficiency (~2%).

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the problems related to environment have emerged because of the revolution in the industrial sectors, mechanization of agricultural fields and cutting-edge technology [1,2]. Nowadays, one of the hottest research topics in the modern world is water pollution due to dyes. In fact, dyes are considered one of the most dangerous problems that threaten human health. Industrial wastes like plastics, rubber industries, leather, textile, and other industries used the dyes as colour agents in their products represent the main sources of dye [1,2]. Yearly, 1000 tonnes of dyes are consumed in the textile industry, in addition to another 100 tonnes are ejected into the water streams. These are considered as one of the most dangerous problems facing the scientific community, where these dyes stay for a long-time in flowing water. This leads to blocking the photosynthetic process, prevents the penetration of sunlight, consumed the dissolved oxygen and reduce the recreation value of stream, which leads to retard the aquatic biota expansion [3]. So, it is essential to invent/innovate methods and techniques with high quality and performance in removing the dyes from wastewater.

It is worth to mention that, there are many traditional approaches for the treatment of body water from pollutants for instance; reverse osmosis, membrane filtration, ozonisation, and chemical precipitation [4–6], however, these approaches are not operationally efficient in eliminating the dye from waste effluent. Also, the coagulation and oxidations by chemical and electrochemical methods are other approaches for eliminating colour effluents but they have shown some drawbacks in large-scale productions because of their expensive prices and economic burdens [7]. Thus the adsorption techniques/approaches are presented to bridge this gap. In terms of recovery, harmfulness, mechanical flexibility, operational simplicity, durability, ease of design, and etc., the adsorption approach/technique was found to be the best when compared to other approaches [8]. So, carbon materials (especially activated carbon) were firstly introduced as an efficient adsorbent material for eliminating the dye from wastewater, however, they were limited in industrial fields because of their high capital running and investment prices [9]. Therefore, the current studies focus on how to develop the carbon materials with commercial features also, producing catalysts having promising surface area, pH, temperature, adsorbent particle size, ratio of adsorbent to adsorbate and optimum adsorbate–adsorbent interaction [9,10].

On the other hand, nano-structures (especially ZnO, TiO2, Fe3O4, etc.) and their nano-composites [11] have captured attention because of their harmless and their abundant. In addition to their high catalytic performance due to their exclusively large surface areas and their pore structure features [12,13]. In spite of the surface area modification of these materials, high adsorption capacity which can be achieved via adjusting their crystallites growth and creating a high porous structure for high adsorption performances [14,15]; but they still show limitations in their practical applications due to their high-energy requirements, difficulty in their fabrication, in-stability against chemical and mechanical tensors, high investment and operational prices, producing secondary toxic pieces, they were also producing small quantities of heavy metals from the large slurries volumes and others [16,17]. So, to overcome these problems, the polymer nano-composites have been introduced as a promising solution. Recently, the polymer nano-composites have been demonstrating promising catalytic efficiency. Particularly, the nano-composites based on polymer/carbon materials or polymer/nano-particles have been extremely used to treat water pollution [18]. This is because of their brilliant adsorption efficiency and their exhibiting micro-structure, which offers huge pore volume and high surface area. However, these nano-composites have several drawbacks and faults for instance, poor properties (like mechanical, electrical and others), poor recycling, weak stability, their costly regeneration, and production of large volumes of sludge [19,20]. So, these nano-composites have been broadly progressing for treating wastewater.

In this literature, rapidly, economically, simply prepared and highly attractive efficient catalysts based on zinc peroxide (ZnO2) and carbon dots decorated zinc peroxide (CZnO2) embedded in polyvinyl alcohol, (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2) catalysts, have been introduced for eliminating dyes from wastewater. In fact, among all the polymeric materials, PVA is recently rated as one of the most important polymers, thanks to its high features and wide range of applications, its simple fabrication, stability against atmospheric conditions, affordability, visibility and others [21–28]. Indeed, the structure and physical features of PVA were changed when doped with various nano-fillers, based on the most recent studies [21–28]. Also, carbon dots (CDots) are recently seemed as an important and unique carbon materials. This is due to their special features (like spin, electronic, optical, photoluminescence features and so on.) boosted by the quantum confinement effects [21–28]. Indeed, CDots were prepared by different approaches for instance, microwave, electrochemical, chemical approaches and laser ablation [21–28] etc., for many practical applications such as photocatalyst, energy conversion, thermoelectric, microwave shielding, cut off UV filters and others [21–28]. On the other hand, in this work, we are fascinated with zinc peroxide (ZnO2) particles in nano-scale, as their structure has two oxygen atoms linked by a single bond. Besides, ZnO2 particles have captured more attention among scientists and academics due to their unique features and potential applications, which arise from the growth of the electronegative behavior of these peroxides and the excess of oxygen behavior [1]. In fact, few-accountable literatures were reported based on ZnO2 and CDots and their polymeric nano-composites for eliminating dyes from wastewater. For instance, El-Shamy [2] designed the nano-composites (PVA/CDots) for eliminating methylene blue dyes from wastewater. It was found that, PVA/CDots system has the ability to remove MB dye from wastewater with removal efficiency (97%) of 30 mg/L of MB within 40 min. Although this system (PVA/CDots) displayed a higher removal efficiency against the MB dyes. However, this shows a limitation in small initial concentration of MB dye (30 mg/L) and weak physical (mechanical and electrical) properties.

Also, El-Shamy [1] fabricated MgO2 nano-rods for eliminating methyl orange dye. This study showed that MgO2 was able to remove (30 mg/L) of methyl orange dye with efficiency (97.4%) within 50 min under the UV light. Also, Yang et al. [29], fabricated ZnO2 particles for eliminating Rhodamine B dyes under the UV light. Moreover, Giannakoudakis et al. [30] prepared ZnO2 particles for mustard gas detoxification. In fact, all these works prove that the metal peroxide materials works only in the alkaline media and their catalytic efficiency limited in acidic and neutral media. In spite these reported studies, the need to acquire catalyst with high adsorbent capacity and high stability and other special requirements is essential. The authors believed that the decoration of ZnO2 nanoparticles with CDots will increase the efficiency in the acidic and neutral media and improve the stability of ZnO2 during the catalytic process.

Thus to reach these aims, we suggest new, simple, eco-friendly, in-expensive and high adsorption capacity nano-composites based on polyvinyl alcohol/zinc peroxide (PVA/ZnO2) and polyvinyl alcohol/carbon dots decorated zinc peroxide (PVA/CZnO2) (see Scheme 1 ), which have a superior catalytic efficiency. Besides, flexibility, durability, mechanical and optical features of PVA, we select PVA in this work due to its functional (−OH) groups. Also, we select glucose to synthesize CDots nano-particles with functional (−COOH/−OH) groups on their surfaces. Also, the excess of oxygen was the important point for selecting ZnO2. Then the casting technique was used to prepare (PVA/ZnO2) and (PVA/CZnO2) nano-composites. This way facilitates the growth of H-bonding (inter/intra H-bonding) via the interaction between PVA through its (-OH) groups and functional (−OH/−COOH) groups on the CDots surfaces and ZnO2. This leads to enhancing the structure, improving the swelling properties and growing the crystallinity of the composite, and developing the pore size and volume; therefore inducing the adsorption process. The obtained results showed that, in addition to the high electrical conductivity (6 ± 1.2 × 10−2 S/cm at 10 wt% of CZnO2) and mechanical parameters of the PVA/CZnO2 nano-composite (Young modulus = 2783.4 ± 30 MPa, Tensile strength = 103.78 ± 5 MPa and Strain at break = 36.51 ± 6) at 10 wt% of CZnO2, it was found, that PVA/CZnO2 catalyst exhibited adsorption capacity towards methylene blue (MB) higher than that of PVA/ZnO2. PVA/CZnO2 catalyst showed the largest adsorption capacity (1972 ± 40 mg/g) at 4 wt% of CZnO2 while PVA/ZnO2 showed (1831 ± 20 mg/g) at the same concentration of ZnO2. Also, the optimum dosage of (PVA/CZnO2) catalyst was 40 mgs. The supreme removal efficiency (>98%) of 2000 mg/L of MB dye was quickly obtained (in 60 min) at the experimental conditions. Langmuir type I model was the adsorption mechanism suggesting the mono-layer MB formation on the surface of catalyst. Also, the chemisorption is the interaction type between the catalysts and molecules of the dye, according to the kinetic model of pseudo-second order. 75% of loaded-dye was desorbed from the catalyst when soaking it in a solution of pH = 1, according to desorption tests. The reused experiments confirmed that the catalyst can be reused for at least 5 runs with minimal loss in its efficiency (~2%). Unique structures and excellent features especially high adsorption capacities all together give these catalysts the opportunity to be unique catalysts with many applications like water treatment desalination and organic dye decomposition.

PVA (M w = 5 × 103 g mol−1, 99.8%) was purchased from the Merck Chemicals, NaOH (99.9% purity), Ethanol (CH3CH2OH, 99.5%), H2O2 (99.8%), Zinc acetate tetetrahydrate (99.9%), D-glucose (99.8%) and acetone (99.9%), were obtained from (Adwic, El-Nasr Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDots, ZnO2 and CZnO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: CDots nano-particles have been synthesized by microwave irradiation method [1,2,21–28]. Also, ZnO2 nano-particles were synthesized via the microwave assisted hydrothermal method [1,2,21–28]. CZnO2 nano-composite was synthesized by using the chemical precipitation method [1,2,21–28]. More details about the preparation of CDots, ZnO2 and CZnO2 nano-particles; see supplementary materials (section S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PVA, PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 nano-composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Casting technique was used to prepare PVA films [1,2,21–28], see section S2. Also, the casting technique was applied to design both PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 nano-composite films [1,2,21–28]. The samples with different ZnO2/CZnO2 concentrations were designed by dissolving ZnO2/CZnO2 in triple-distilled water. After that, ZnO2/CZnO2 solution was dropped into PVA solution. The mixture was left for 2 h to obtain a high dispersion of ZnO2/CZnO2 in PVA template. The mixture was poured in glass dishes and dried in air conditions for three days to evaporate water.

XRD analyses for the samples were performed using Brucker Axs-D8 diffractometer. JEOL-JEM-1230 TEM microscope was applied at 120 kV to evaluate ZnO2/CZnO2 particle size. Micromeritics ASAP 2020 USA BET instrument was conducted to study the surface area and porous structure of the catalysts under a liquid N2 atmosphere. The FESEM was applied to scan the morphology of the nano-composite with a model: JEOL JSM- 6500F, using an electron beam with a current of 40 nA and energy 15 keV. The mechanical parameters were measured by an AMETEK mechanical apparatus. Also, DC conductivity of the nano-composite films was measured using a Keithley 642 programmable electrometer at room temperature. Thermal DSC studies were performed via (DSC-60 Shimadzu) model. The measurements were carried out under N2 stream with a flow rate (20 ml/min) from T = 29–290 °C at heating rate 10 °C/min, (more details see section S3).

The swelling degree (%) of the nano-composites was calculated as follow [2,27]: 20 mgs of the nano-composite samples were weighed in a dry (Wd) and then soaked in deionized water at ambient temperature for 24 h. After, the excess of water was removed from the surface of the swollen films by wiping them with tissue paper, the samples were weighed (Ws). Then, after doing the water dipping experiment, the nano-composites were dried for 10 h at 60 °C. The swelling degree (%) and the weight loss (%) of the nano-composites were calculated from the relations:

Indeed, it is vital to evaluate the point of zero charge (pHPZC) of the applied catalyst. The pH-drift approach [31] was applied to evaluate the value of pHPZC. In this experiment, a solution of 0.1 M NaCl (100 mL) was put in quartz container at ambient temperature under mechanical stirrer (rotation rate 200 rpm). The values of pH were tuned between 2 and 10 by using a 0.1 M of NaOH/HCl solution. Then 100 mgs of the catalyst was inserted into the solution and then the solution was left for 24 h under a mechanical stirrer. The final value of pH (pHfinal) was evaluated and the obtained results were drawn with the initial value of pH (pHinitial). The value of pH at which the initial and final values are equal (pHfinal = pHinitial) or (pHfinal-pHinitial = zero), this value is equal to that of pHPZC.

The experiments of the adsorption process were done in 250 mL vials. These vials were filled with 50 mL of methylene blue aliquots with mechanical stirring (200 rpm) at ambient temperature. The catalyst was injected into the vials/flasks. Then at a specified time intervals, 5 mL of solution was withdrawn and (centrifuged at 3000 rpm to separate the catalyst) measuring the absorbance at λ max = 665 nm by using UV–Vis spectroscopy (Jasco, V500) to scrutinize the adsorption process. The absorbance indicates the residual concentration of MB dye. The given analytical curve is: Absorbance = 0.119 [MB] − 0.079 with R2 ~ 0.999. The adsorption capacity (
q e
) in (mg/g) was determined at different times from the relation:

Where Co and Ce in (mg/L) are the initial and the final concentration of dye in solution at equilibrium, respectively, V is the volume of solution in (L) and m is the mass of catalyst on a dry basis in (g). All trails were repeated for three times (n = 3) and then the mean value and standard deviation were determined.

After the catalysts were collected, they were rinsed with deionized water to eliminate the dye molecules that are not adsorbed on the catalysts. Then these catalysts were injected into flasks filled with deionized water. The experiment was done with changing pH values for 1 h at ambient temperature. By using the absorption spectra, the desorbed quantity of dye was determined as stated above. The efficiency of dye desorption was determined from the equation (2):

Where Cads and Cdes in (mg) are the quantities of adsorbed dye on the catalyst and recovered from the dye-loaded catalyst.

After the regeneration of catalysts, they were used in repeated adsorption-desorption cycles. For the recycling test, at first, 40 mgs of catalysts were soaked in 50 mL of 2000 mg/L of dye solution under mechanical stirrer with 100 rpm for 60 min. The test was performed at pH = 6, 0.1 M of ionic strength and ambient temperature. Then, the dye-loaded catalyst was collected and soaked in 0.1 M HCl (50 mL) under a mechanical stirrer (200 rpm) at ambient temperature for 60 min. Then, 50 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution was applied to regenerate the adsorption sites on the surface of catalyst for 30 min. The catalyst was washed with deionized water and applied in a new adsorption/desorption run. After each run, the values of
q e
were determined.

TEM microscope was performed to confirm the decoration of CDots on ZnO2. From TEM images, Fig. (1a) , it can be seen that the size of CDots particle is in the nano-scale with a uniform spherical shape. As seen, the size distribution of CDots was statistically estimated and it was found in between 5 and 11 nm. The mean size of CDots was found to be 8.7 ± 4 nm. Also, Fig 1b proves the decoration of CDots on the surface of ZnO2 with average particle size of ZnO2 = 22.6 ± 3 nm.

XRD patterns of ZnO2 and CZnO2 nano-particles were carried out to confirm the formation and decoration of CDots on the surface of ZnO2 (inset of Fig. 1c and d). As seen, Five diffraction patterns were detected at 2θ = 31.61, 36.79, 53.17, 63.21° and 66.4° for the (hkl) planes (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1) and (222) of the ZnO2 particles (space group: Pa3, JCPDS 13–0311, ZnO2) [29]. Besides these patterns, an additional pattern was detected at 2θ = 23° for C(002) of hexagonal lattice structure of CDots for the CZnO2 nanoparticle sample [2,21–28].

Also, the FESEM images of the ZnO2 and CZnO2 nanoparticles were performed (Fig. 1c and d). First sight to the FESEM images, the spherical shapes of both ZnO2 and CZnO2 nanoparticles were observed. Also, the ZnO2 and CZnO2 nanoparticles are uniformly distributed without any agglomeration or aggregation. Moreover, it was noticed that the particle size of both ZnO2 and CZnO2 nanoparticles is smaller than 50 nm (Fig. 1c). Also, there is no change in the particle size was observed after decorating the CDots on the ZnO2 surface (the morphology remained without any change in shape or size). However, it was observed that some dense structures in the CZnO2 sample were created as a result of the decoration process (Fig. 1d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD,BET and SEM analysis of the nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig 2 a presents XRD peaks of PVA, PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 films at ambient temperature. As seen, there is a peak at 2θ = 19.5° for hkl plane (101) of PVA polymer, which is produced from the strong hydrogen H-bonding (inter/intramolecular type) between PVA molecular chains in the crystalline regions of the semi-crystalline PVA molecules [1,2,21–28]. Besides that, in XRD spectra of PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 films, the reflection peaks of ZnO2 nano-particles were observed [29]. In order to study the effect of ZnO2/CZnO2 on the PVA structure, the average crystallite sizes D are estimated using the XRD data for pure PVA as well as PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 nanocomposites from the Scherer's equation [1,2,21–28]. The results of the calculated crystallite size are tabulated in the Table (S1). From this table, it is clear that the width at half maximum intensity β increased with the presence of ZnO2/CZnO2 nanoparticles and hence the crystallites size decreases sharply with dopant. Also, there are a shift in a peak position of the (101) plane to a lower angles when embedding ZnO2/CZnO2 in the PVA. This behavior may be attributed to strain in the samples due to the change in the crystal size. The d-spacing is calculated using Bragg's law equation,
2 d
sin
θ = n λ
[1,2,21–28]. The calculated d-spacing are 4.98, 5.76 and 6.12 A° for pure PVA, PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2, respectively. There is a shift in the d-spacing, this shift attributed to the shift in 2θ which causing a shift in d-spacing. Also, the average inter-crystallite separation (R) in the amorphous region of the sample was evaluated from the position of the maximum of the halo
R =

[1,2,21–28]. From the above equation the calculated distance between the hydroxyl groups of crystalline PVA is 5.46 A°. The average inter-crystallite separation in the nanocomposite films are 5.50 A° and 5.54 A° according to PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 films [1,2,21–28]. Also, the crystallinity of the polymeric nanocomposites was studied [1,2,21–28]. In fact, crystallinity degree is the integrated area under the crystalline regions divided by the summation of both the integrated areas of the crystalline and amorphous regions according to the relation;
χ =

A c

A a
)
, where Ac is the integrated area under the crystalline part and Aa is the integrated area under the amorphous part. The crystallinity of the films was determined, and it was found 40.75 ± 1%, 44.43 ± 2% and 48.37 ± 2% for PVA, PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2, respectively. The increase in the crystallinity is attributed to the growth of the number of H-bonding (inter/intra types) between ZnO2 nano-particles and PVA chains. Also, it is worth to mention that the crystallinity of the nano-composite increases when decorating ZnO2 with CDots and embedding CZnO2 in PVA template. This is due to the presence of (–COOH/–OH) groups on the surface of CZnO2 which interact with PVA through (–OH) groups and hence increasing the number of H-bonding, causing the reordering of the chains in the nanocomposite as a result increasing the crystallinity of the nano-composite (Scheme 1), (more details about crystallinity, see supplementary materials section S4).

Also, FE-SEM micrograph was performed to study the morphology (Fig. 2b and c); it was observed that CZnO2/ZnO2 particles were arranged uniformly in PVA template with a high density. This way of arrangement allows avoiding the agglomeration of CZnO2/ZnO2 in PVA matrix, Fig. 2b. As seen, some large voids and holes were observed inside the template of the nano-composite films, Fig. 2c, these voids help in increasing the adsorption capacity especially in the PVA/CZnO2 nano-composite.

Fig. 2d,e shows N2 adsorption-desorption analysis of PVA/CZnO2 and PVA/ZnO2 nano-composites, which reveals the isotherm type for both catalysts is (type IV) with hysteresis loop of type H3. This is due to the prevalence of meso-pores structure. As seen, PVA/CZnO2 nano-composite has a specific surface area (241.04 m2 g-1) larger than PVA/ZnO2 (167.17 m2 g-1). Indeed, the high surface area improves the catalytic efficiency of the nano-composite. The BJH method was used to show the distributions of pore volumes and size [1,2,32]. It is worth to mention that the two catalysts have the average pore size (15.16 nm) for PVA/CZnO2 and (17.08 nm) for PVA/ZnO2 (Table 1 ), in addition, the total pore volumes were found to be 0.71 and 0.74 cm3/g for PVA/CZnO2 and PVA/ZnO2, respectively. As a result, these results show the catalysts having a mixture of meso and micro-porous nature. This behavior plays a significant role in enhancing the adsorption capacity of both catalysts.

The swelling degree (%) and the weight loss were measured to study the water uptake behavior of the two catalysts by using the water dipping experiment [2,27] (Fig. 3 a and b). It is worth to mention that pure PVA has a swelling degree (%) larger than the two nano-composites. This is attributed to the existence of (−OH) groups in the backbone of PVA polymer that is accountable for the hydrophilicity of PVA. But these (−OH) groups were consumed during the interaction of PVA with ZnO2 and/or CZnO2, as a result PVA is less able to create H-bonding with H2O, producing the small swelling degree (%) [2,27]. Also, it is observed that with increasing CZnO2 concentration the swelling degree (%) of PVA/CZnO2 nano-composites decreases. This is due to the existence of CZnO2 (CDots) in PVA template which increases the rigidification behavior of the nano-composite as a result, declining the free volume and mobility of PVA segments/chains. Also, the efficient H-bonding that is produced through the reaction of CZnO2 and PVA (via CDots on ZnO2 surface) decreases the hydrophilic nature of PVA, consequently, PVA is not able to make an H-bonding with H2O and this leads to declining the swelling degree (%) of PVA/CZnO2 nano-composites [2,27]. Indeed, PVA is slowly dissolved in cool water and quickly dissolved in warm water. CDots (via −OH) interacted with PVA (via −OH groups) and a strong H-bonding will be formed in the whole PVA, thus PVA becomes water insoluble. Polyvinyl alcohol can make a hydrogen bonding with H2O through its (−OH) groups, thus PVA is able to absorb a great quantity of water, and as a result PVA can swell. Also, Fig. 3b reveals the weight loss (%) of the obtained nano-composites. It is worth to mention, 0% is the weight loss of the PVA/CZnO2 nano-composites, which means that these nanocomposites are entirely water insoluble and they can be applied in the applications that based on water [2,27]. Similar behavior was observed in previous works [2,27].

These results were supported by studying the surface morphology of (PVA/CZnO2) films by SEM analysis (Fig. 3c–f). As seen, a high distribution and good dispersion of CZnO2 nano-particles in the PVA template were observed with a high degree of homogeneity. Thanks to the assembling of CZnO2 nano-particles in the films during the process of solvent evaporation, CZnO2 have a slightly aggregation and agglomeration, showing a spherical shaping on the films surface; Fig. 3c-e. CZnO2 nanoparticles have good compatibility and adequate dispersion in PVA template with a slightly agglomeration and aggregation with increasing CZnO2 concentration, thanks to the strong H-bonding interaction between CZnO2 and PVA [2,27]. Percolating networks of CZnO2 were formed that is accountable for the presence of spherical shape and therefore improving the mechanical features. SEM micrograph for (PVA/CZnO2) catalyst shows a fine distribution and high adhesion property between CZnO2 and PVA polymer in the whole matrix of the polymer [2,27]. As seen, with increasing CZnO2 in the nano-composite, this leads to producing a high packing density of CZnO2 inside the template and improving the inter-particle connectivity (like particle-to-particle behavior) and the aspect ratio parameter was enhanced [2,27]. These factors work together to improve the thermal, mechanical properties and the electrical conductivity of the nano-composites [2,27].

From that expects, the conductivity of the prepared films was measured (Fig. 4 a). Fig. 4a shows the electrical conductivity of both PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 catalysts. As seen, with increasing the concentration of CZnO2 nano-particles in PVA template, the conductivity increases. It was found that the conductivity of PVA/CZnO2 (6 ± 1.2 × 10−2 S/cm at 10 wt% of CZnO2) was higher than the conductivity of PVA/ZnO2 (2.5 ± 1 × 10−3 S/cm) at the same concentration. This is attributed to the decoration of ZnO2 surface with a highly conductive CDots, which is responsible for connecting PVA chains with each other and making a conductive path, and hence increasing the conductivity [2,18–21]. In fact, the catalysts with high conductive behavior have the advantage to remove the dyes due to high charge transfer behavior and enhance free conduction electrons, which leads to improving the catalytic activity.

Also, the mechanical behavior of the nanocomposite was studied (Fig. 4b). It was found that young modulus and tensile strength of pure PVA were 1483.7 ± 35 MPa and 36.17 ± 6 MPa, respectively. As seen, with embedding the ZnO2 nanoparticles in the PVA template, both young modulus and tensile strength were enhanced to be 2420.5 ± 17 MPa and 83.72 ± 6 MPa at 10 wt% of ZnO2 nanoparticles. On the other hand, with growing the CZnO2 concentration, young's modulus and the tensile strength were increased to be 2783.4 ± 30 and 103.78 ± 5 at 10 wt% of CZnO2, respectively, however the Strain at break decreases, as shown in Table S2. The higher growth in Young's modulus and strength at break can be attributed to the growth of the packing density of the ZnO2/CZnO2 nano-particles in the PVA template. Where the ZnO2/CZnO2 particles were strongly interacted with polyvinyl alcohol chain, this producing a reduction in the strain of the lattice under an external tensile stress [10]. Moreover, the increasing in the CZnO2 concentration inside the PVA template leads to producing a charge transfer complex (CTCs) by growing the molecular hydrogen forces (intra/inter types), this prevents the PVA macromolecules from sliding over each other. Furthermore, the neck region was shifted to low strain values, and the neck was gradually appeared with the growth of the CZnO2 concentration. In fact, the growth of the crosslinking density (intra-intermolecular forces) inside the nano-composite with growing the CZnO2 concentration was the reason behind the observation of such behavior. The above observations indicate that by increasing CZnO2 concentration, the partially crystalline PVA is becoming more crystalline. These results agree with the XRD, DSC and water up-take results. Also, silmilar results were observed in Refs. [1,2,21–28]. Moreover, the reduction in the strain at break is elucidated as a result of doping PVA with CZnO2, the chemical bonding formation between PVA chains and CZnO2 leads to raising the crosslinking density that is reflected in a decrease in strain at break.

On the other hand, the thermal properties of the obtained films were examined using DSC calorimeter (Fig. 4c). Three endothermic peaks were detected for all films. For the PVA sample, the 1st peak (83.2–94.4 °C) indicates the glass temperature (Tg) of the PVA structural relaxation produced from the random motion (micro-Brownian) of the PVA chains [1,2,21–28]. The 2nd peak (214.6 °C) was assigned to the melting point (Tm), which indicates the semi-crystalline nature of PVA. Moreover, the 3rd peak at 224.6 °C is related to the decomposition temperature (Td). Embedding ZnO2 nano-particles into the PVA polymer causes an increase in the Tglass peak of the nano-composite. This increase in Tglass is due to increasing in the number of inter-intramolecular H-bonding between ZnO2 and PVA chain [1,2,21–28]. Also, when doping PVA polymer with CZnO2 nano-particles, the Tglass peak increases due to the increase in the cross-linking density by increasing the inter-intramolecular H-bonding between (–COOH/–OH) groups) on the surface of CZnO2 and PVA chain through (–OH) groups. It is exciting to observe that the position of Tglass peak is slightly shifted to higher temperatures, which indicates the higher thermal stability of the nano-composites. The similar observation of the increase in the glass (Tglass) temperature of PVA matrix was reported in many experiments [1,2,21–28]. The melting point (Tm) of the nano-composite increased with embedding ZnO2/CZnO2 nano-particles. This increase in (Tm) is attributed to the modifications of the microstructure, crystal orientation, and crystallinity of the nano-composite when embedding ZnO2/CZnO2 into PVA, also by the change in the morphology of PVA (See SEM image) and its crystalline structure, as well. This leads increasing the lamellar structure (both lamella width and thickness) through the crystallization process [1,2,21–28]. Also, entering ZnO2/CZnO2 nano-particles in the interspacing of PVA increases the local ordering of PVA, the crystal perfection and increases the heat resistance [1,2,21–28]. Also, (Td) decomposition temperature of the films increased with the growth of ZnO2/CZnO2 concentration. This is due to the intermolecular interaction between PVA and ZnO2/CZnO2 that could strengthen the molecular backbones of the films, which may be a direct result of its rigid nature. Now, it is interesting to study the crystallinity degree of the films to understand the change in the structure of the films by adding ZnO2/CZnO2 nano-particles. From the heat-of-fusion, the degree of crystallinity can be calculated through the equation [1,2,21–28];
X c
= Δ
H f
/ W
H
f , 100
, where ʌHƒ is the heat-of-fusion for PVA, ʌHƒ,100 = 160 J/g is the heat-of-fusion for 100% crystalline PVA, and W is the weight fraction of PVA. The crystallinity (χc) of the nano-composites was calculated and it was found 39.58 ± 2%, 45.33 ± 3% and 49.12 ± 2% for PVA, PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 films, respectively. As seen, these values agree with the values of crystallinity obtained from XRD results.

Fig. 5 a shows the impact of CZnO2 concentration on the adsorption of the dye. As seen, the value of qe raised from 1842 ± 8 for (CZnO2 = 2 wt%) to 1972 ± 40 mg/g for (CZnO2 = 4 wt%), these values are higher than that for 4 wt% of ZnO2 (1805 ± 5 mg/g). As proven, the morphology of the catalyst was affected by CZnO2 (i.e. larger pore volume) where (-OH and -COOH) groups on CZnO2 surface facilitate the interactions between the dye molecules and catalyst [2]. As seen, the adsorption capacity (mg/g) decreases at higher concentrations of CZnO2. When the concentration of CZnO2 exceeds above 4 wt%, the larger number of interactions between PVA and CZnO2 restricts the expansion of network and limits the diffusion of liquid through the nano-composite. Moreover, the adsorption sites number existing on the surface of the catalyst reduces, as more interactions (dipolar–dipolar type) between PVA (via, -OH groups) and CZnO2 (-COOH/-OH groups) spread out. As the optimal composition, the composite at 4 wt% of CZnO2 was utilized in the following experiments.

In fact, the scientific principle upon which the process of adsorption was based is the mass transfer. Thus the materials that have the capability to remove a large amount of adsorbate (dyes) at low concentrations/dosages are considered promising catalyst (adsorbent materials). This property is one of the most important parameters in the catalysis technology due to its ability to decrease the threats linked to the secondary effluence and decrease the costs of experiments [2,33]. From that point, the impact of the PVA/CZnO2 catalyst dosage on the removal efficiency was studied (Fig. 5b). As seen, the elimination of dye was clearly raised with increasing the dosage of the catalyst from 10 to 40 mgs. More interestingly, the removal efficiency of the dye was 98% under the optimum concentration condition of PVA/CZnO2 catalyst (i.e. 40 mgs). Overall, with increasing in the concentration/dose of PVA/CZnO2 up to 40 mgs, the adsorption sites (surface area) increases, this leads to producing more and more interactions between methylene blue dye and PVA/CZnO2 (dye-catalyst) and hence increasing the adsorption. Many literatures reported the similar behavior [2,34]. Alternatively, small removal efficiency was observed when the concentrations/doses of PVA/CZnO2 exceeded from (>40 mg). This is because of the aggregation behavior produced at higher PVA/CZnO2 concentrations/dose where the surface interaction between the particles of catalyst/adsorbent leads to a reduction in the available/free adsorption sites number and, as a result, reducing the efficiency of adsorption [2,35]. Also, the reduction in the values of
q e
with increasing the dosage/concentration of catalyst can be linked with the sites remaining without unsaturation (unsaturated sites) [2,35]. For knowledge, the additional experiments were done using the catalyst concentration of 40 mgs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of pH on the adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para:
In fact, the process of adsorption is affected by changing pH value. This is attributed to the ionization of distributed functional groups located on the catalyst. The adsorbent-adsorbate (catalyst-dye) equilibrium was altered when the functional groups are charged; as a result, the adsorption mechanisms and its kinetics are altered [2,36]. Fig. 6 a, shows that both catalysts have the same behavior of adsorption. But PVA/CZnO2 exhibited the large values of (
q e
) of all tested conditions. It is clear that, the adsorption of MB dye increased when increasing the values of pH from 2 to 6. Where the value of
q e
increased by 50% when the value of pH increased from 2 to 6 for the two catalysts/adsorbents. Interestingly, no change on the adsorption capacity was observed for the two catalysts/adsorbents at the value of pH ≥ 8 (near the neutral and alkaline media). In fact, the adsorption of MB dyes as a cationic class dye on a solid catalyst is motivated by ionic interaction. So, the strength and nature of the interactions between the molecules of MB dye and the adsorption sites are controlled by the charges on the surface of the catalyst/adsorbent. During the study of dye adsorption process, it is important to evaluate (PZC) value because this factor indicates pH value that denoting that the catalyst surface is free of charge. Also, it is easy to predict the charge of the catalyst surface in a certain condition of pH from pHPZC. These investigations are valuable, which allow exploring the interactions nature (electrostatic type) between the catalyst and the organic pieces participated in the adsorption [2,36]. In fact, the molecules of cationic dyes were adsorbed when (pHPZC)catalyst < (pH)solution, but the molecules of anionic dyes were adsorbed when pHPZC)catalyst > (pH)solution [2,36]. As seen before, the values of pHPZC for PVA/ZnO2 = 6.29 and for PVA/CZnO2 = 6.60 (Fig. 6b). The observed variation on the values of pHPZC can be elucidated by (OH) groups of PVA, which increase the interaction between acidic pieces (e.g. H2O+) and the surface of catalyst. But at pH < pHPZC (acidic environments), the efficiency of MB dye adsorption on PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 catalysts was small. This is because of the protonation produced for a large quantity of the functional groups that exist on the surface of the catalyst/adsorbent, thus decreasing the interaction between the catalyst/adsorbent and the molecules of MB dye. In addition, the acidic medium in the solution provides more H+ ions leads to more competition between the ions of electrolyte and MB dyes for the sites of adsorption of catalysts. Alternatively, at pH > pHPZC or under alkaline environments (near the neutral medium) the deprotonation of (OH) groups of PVA resulted in increasing the negatively charged groups on the adsorbents and hence increasing the adsorption of MB dye. Also, -ve charged groups on the surface of catalyst improved the repulsion type of electrostatic forces (anion–anion type), enlarged the catalyst network and increased the adsorption of MB dye molecules [2,36] (Fig. 6c). From the obtained results, the best performance of dye adsorption on PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 was produced at the pH value near pH of natural water. This behavior gives these catalysts a practical feature. This is because of the obtained catalysts are not needed for adjustment or treatment before the adsorption process. It is worth to mention that the optimum value of pH for adsorbing dye molecules on PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 was 6.

In fact, the interaction between dye molecules and the catalyst was accompanied by studying the kinetics isotherms of dye adsorption on the catalysts. In fact, there are many models describing the kinetics isotherms of dye adsorption on the catalysts such as Elovich, intra-particle diffusion and pseudo-first and pseudo-second orders kinetic models [2,37,38]. The linear form of the pseudo-first order model was suggested by Lagergren as follow:

Frist sight to data tabulated in table leads us to the following remark: (1) the kinetic model of pseudo-second order is well fitted with the experimental data of the dye molecules adsorbed on PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 catalysts, since the values of (R2 > 0.999), in addition to the agreement/correlation between the values of calculated

. In fact, the pseudo-second order supposes that the adsorption process adjusted by the rate-limiting step of chemisorption interaction [2,39]. In this case, it is assumed that valence forces are participated because of the electrons exchanging or sharing between the catalyst and the dye [2,39]. Also, the rate of adsorption depends on the capacity of sorption not on the catalyst/adsorbate concentration, according to this kinetic model [2,39]. The error function, non-linear Chi-square (χ2), is determined from the equation (8). This function (χ2) was applied to clarify which the best kinetic models is fitted to the results obtained experimentally. Generally, the number of χ2 is small when the experimental data are similar to the data obtained from the models. As seen from the given results in Table 2, the pseudo-first order gives values of χ2 much larger than those of pseudo-second order. Thus, the model which is high-fitted to the experimental results is the pseudo-second order model (Fig. 7c).

The obtained values of (t1/2and ki) are tabulated in Table 2, proving that the decoration of CDots on ZnO2 and inserting CZnO2 into the matrix of polymer improved the rate of dye adsorption and reduced the half-time of CZnO2 when compared to ZnO2 only. Also, the intra-particle diffusion model was studied in (Fig. 7e). It is worth mentioning that the intra-particle diffusion participates in the 2nd stage of the process of dye adsorption but the contrary occurs in the 1st and 3rd stages according to their lower R2 values. In the 2nd stage, the adsorption of dye accelerates showing non-sequential diffusion of molecules of dye into the larger micro-pores within the catalysts as reported in Ref. [40]. In fact, the obtained data propose that dye adsorption in the catalyst pores is favored than the dye adsorption by intra-particle diffusion [40].

Using the relations between Ce/qe and Ce for type I of Langmuir and 1/qe versus 1/Ce for type II of Langmuir, the constants of Langmuir isotherm are determined (Fig. 8c and d). Also, by using the relation (qe vs. ln Ce), the constants of Temkin are determined (Fig. 8e) and from the relation (ln qe vs. ε 2), the constants of Dubinin-Radushkevich are determined (Fig. 8f). Table 3 shows all the obtained parameters of isotherms and their (R2) values.

Table 3 shows that the best-fitting to the experimental data is the model of Langmuir isotherm (type I) when compared to the other models of isotherm bearing in mind the values of R2 and the agreements between the values of
q

( max )

. It is clear that the other models of isotherms were insufficient to elucidate the adsorption process. It important to drive attention that, the two catalysts exhibited amazing/high adsorption capacities q(max). The adsorption of dyes on the surface of catalysts is accompanied with the monolayer formation of dyes on the catalyst surface, according to the Langmuir isotherm. Also, the surface of adsorbents shows morphology with a homogenous property with a limited quantity of adsorption sites [41].

In fact, the constant, kL(I), of Langmuir type I is correlated to the energy participated in the adsorption. Generally, high values of kL(I) refer a large attraction among the molecules of adsorbate and the sites of adsorption on the surface of catalyst. The constant kL(I) of the catalyst (PVA/CZnO2) was calculated and it was found to be larger than that of PVA/ZnO2. This indicates that the CDots improved the interaction between the molecules of dye and the catalyst. A dimensionless constant or separation factor (RL) was also determined by using the Langmuir isotherm, which is the equilibrium parameter and linked to the adsorption propitiousness and can be determined from the equation (18):
(18)

In fact, the values of RL parameter refer to the adsorption process. If RL = 1 the adsorption is linear, if 0<RL < 1 it is favorable, if RL > 1 it is unfavorable, and irreversible if RL = 0 [2]. The values of RL for PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 were calculated (Table 3) and it was found that the dye adsorption process on these catalysts was favorable. It is worth to mention, the value of RL in PVA/CZnO2 is lower/smaller than that in PVA/ZnO2. Table 4 introduces the comparison between the adsorption performance of dye on the nano-composites prepared in the present study and other similar works.

Fig. 9 a shows the relation between the adsorption capacity (
q e
) and temperature. As seen, with increasing the temperature, the values of (
q e
) decreases for the two catalysts. It is worth to mention that 5% decrease in the values of the adsorption capacities for the two catalysts when the temperatures changed from 25 to 50 °C. These results in this effort refer that the obtained catalysts have a huge practical application in eliminating the MB dye at ambient conditions. Three main reasons behind the decreasing in the values of (
q e
) at high temperatures: (i) the solubility of the dye increases with high temperatures and the interactions between the solvent and catalyst inhibits the adsorption; (ii) the high temperature raises the kinetic energy of the materials included in the adsorption process, which makes the interactions between the adsorbent-adsorbate very weak; and (iii) the high temperature increases the molecules mobility of the dye through the solution and affects their adsorption. In fact, similar results were described in many works such as [42]. Here in this section, we calculated the equilibrium thermodynamics parameters related to the adsorption of dye. These thermodynamics parameters were determined from the equations:
(19)

By combining the equations (19) and (20), the resultant is the relation of van't Hoff,

Using the relation (ln Kd vs. 1/T), it is easy to calculate the ΔH° and ΔS° parameters (Fig. 9b). Table 5 shows the obtained thermodynamic parameters. It is worth to mention, when the values of ΔG° are negative, this refers to the spontaneous adsorption of dye on PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 at the different environments of temperature. But at the ambient temperature (298 K), the adsorption appears to be more spontaneous proving the obtained results in Fig. 9a. In reality, the physical adsorption (i.e. physisorption) process takes place when the values of ΔG° are between 0 to −20 kJ/mol, based on the literature [43]. Also, the values of ΔH° are negative indicating that adsorption on (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2) is exothermic process. Moreover, the low values of ΔH° indicate the participated energies in the dye adsorption on (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2) are weak proving the physical nature of this process [43].

As seen from Table 5, the negative values of ΔS° for (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2) refer to the rearrangement of adsorption on the surfaces of (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2). Generally, the homogeneity of the surface region increases as the molecules of dye are adsorbed on PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 while no considerable changes happened in their internal structure [2,43]. The addition of CZnO2 to the matrix of PVA stimulated a significant change on the thermodynamics parameters. Generally, the obtained changes proved that both CDots and ZnO2 played a significant role on dye adsorption.

Fig. 10 a shows the experiments of desorption process at various pH environments using the dye-loaded PVA/CZnO2 catalyst. Fig. 10a shows that the largest values of dye desorption were realized at pH < 3 (strongly acidic media), alternatively, the release of dye is inefficient near the neutral or in alkaline media (<20%). It is worth to mention that 75% was the efficiency of dye desorption at (pH = 1). The above results proved the strong interaction between the surface of catalyst and the molecules of dyes [44].

Fig. 10b shows the reuse experiments of five runs of adsorption/desorption process for regenerating PVA/CZnO2 catalyst. The obtained results showed that the capacity of adsorption of PVA/CZnO2 remained constant without change for along 5 runs. For more accuracy, 2% decrease in the values of
q e
(mg/g) was observed between the 1st and 5th run. Apparently, the obtained results proved that PVA/CZnO2 has the ability to work as a catalyst with high-efficiency for dye removal from wastewater. Finally, the results obtained from the current study prove that these nano-composites (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2) have attractive features (e.g. recycling, cheap, flexibility, fast adsorption kinetics, handling and large adsorption capacity) that encourage their practical applications in wastewater management and desalination.
The strength of the ionic (NaCl and CaCl2) electrolytes on the adsorption capacity (mg/g) of the (PVA/CZnO2) catalyst was studied (Fig. 10c).

Fig. 10c shows that the adsorbed capacity of MB dye was strongly influenced by the change in the ionic strength and the presence of electrolytes. The value of
q e
was calculated in 0.5 mol/L of NaCl solution and it was found to be 1630 ± 27 mg/g but it was found 1475 ± 20 mg/g in the CaCl2 solution. These obtained results were lower than the obtained value (1972 ± 40 mg/g) without electrolytes. It is observed that, with the increasing of the ionic strength, the adsorption MB molecule on the catalyst decrease. This behavior can be elucidated by the competition between the molecules of MB dyes and the Na+/Ca2+ ions. These Na+/Ca2+ ions are easily attached to the anionic sites which are adsorbed on the surface of PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 at a strong strength of ionic electrolytes, which produces a decrease in the adsorption capacity of MB molecules. Thanks to the strong strength of ionic electrolytes, the shielding effect is also contributed to a reduction in the adsorption capacity of MB molecules [1,2,45]. Also, the spare of Na+/Ca2+ ions will dimerize the molecules of MB dyes and hence decreasing their electrostatic nature as reported by some studies [1,2,45]. Interestingly, the adsorption capacity of MB molecules in CaCl2 solution was lower than NaCl under the identical environments of experiment. This can be attributed to the ions of Ca2+ have a strong impact on the adsorption of dye because of their high charge density [2,45]. These results are similar to other earlier published works including MB dye adsorption on solid catalysts [1,2,45].

The proposed mechanism of MB adsorption onto the catalyst is sketched in Fig. 11 . Based on the functional groups existing on the surface CZnO2, the adsorption mechanism of methylene blue can be attributed to the number of interactions:

(i) H-bonding interaction between the functional groups (-OH and/or -COOH) on the surface of CZnO2 and nitrogen atoms of methylene blue. (ii) Electrostatic interactions (attraction types) between the negatively charged functional groups on CZnO2 surface with positively charged sites (methylene blue cations) that are strongly adsorbed from the solution. (iii) π–π stacking interactions between aromatic rings of MB and the CDots on the surface of ZnO2.

In this study, two nano-composites (PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2) have been successfully fabricated for catalytic applications. XRD, TEM, SEM and BET were used to study the structure and morphology of the catalysts. The morphology of the nano-composite was altered by embedding both ZnO2 and CZnO2 nano-particles in the matrix of PVA, according to SEM images. The expected H-bonding creation between PVA chains and the nano-fillers leads to an increase in the crystallinity, mechanical parameters, and electrical conductivity and also develops the pore size and volume, which is reflected in increasing the performance of adsorption. PVA/CZnO2 catalyst displayed ultra-high adsorption capacity against methylene blue (MB). Importantly, at the same concentration (4 wt%) of each filler, PVA/CZnO2 catalyst showed the largest adsorption capacity (1972 ± 40 mg/g) where PVA/ZnO2 (1831 ± 20 mg/g), proving the important role of CDots on the adsorption process. The supreme removal efficiency (>98%) of 2000 mg/L of MB dye was quickly obtained (in 60 min) at the experimental conditions. Langmuir type I model was the adsorption mechanism suggesting MB dye monolayer creation on the surface of catalyst. Chemisorption is the interaction type between the catalyst and molecules of dye, according to the kinetic model of pseudo-second order. 4 wt% of CZnO2, 40 mgs of nano-composite, low ionic strength (0.1 mol/L of electrolyte), pH = 6, and room temperatures, all the optimum conditions for the adsorption of methylene blue dye on the catalysts. It is worth to mention that 75% of loaded-dye was recovered from the catalyst by soaking it in a solution of pH = 1 according to desorption experiments. For along five recycles, the PVA/CZnO2 catalyst showed high stability without losing its efficiency during the experimental conditions according the obtained recycling experiments. Finally, the obtained results rank PVA/ZnO2 and PVA/CZnO2 catalysts as candidate adsorbent materials for wastewater management, desalination and catalytic applications.
Ahmed Gamal El-Shamy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.
